name: Nylas Calendar and Email API
what_it_does: Nylas provides a unified REST API that connects to Gmail, Outlook, Exchange, and other calendar/email providers, allowing agents to read and send email, manage calendar events, and query availability across any connected user account via a single consistent interface.
use_cases: Reading and sending emails on behalf of users across Gmail, Outlook, and Exchange from an agent
Creating, updating, and canceling calendar events on connected user calendars
Querying free/busy availability across multiple user calendars for scheduling automation
Triggering agent workflows in response to new emails or calendar event changes via webhooks
Automating meeting scheduling by checking availability and creating events on the correct provider
not_for: Applications that only need to integrate with a single email provider (use provider-native APIs)
High-volume email sending at marketing scale (use SendGrid or Mailgun instead)
Agents that do not need to act on behalf of end users with connected accounts
best_when: An agent needs to manage calendar or email across multiple providers (Gmail, Outlook, Exchange) without maintaining separate integrations for each one.
avoid_when: You only need a single provider integration or are building bulk email delivery — Nylas adds cost and complexity over direct provider APIs in those cases.

auth.notes: Agent uses a Nylas API key (Bearer token) for server-to-server calls. End users connect their calendar/email accounts via an OAuth flow that returns a grant ID. All API calls reference the grant ID to act on behalf of that user. Scopes control read vs. write access to calendar and email.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free tier: up to 5 connected accounts for development
pricing.paid_tiers: Starter from $0.01 per connected account per month; Production tiers with volume pricing; Enterprise custom
pricing.notes: Pricing is per connected user account per month. Development accounts are free. Production pricing scales with the number of end-user accounts connected, which can grow significantly in multi-tenant applications.

performance.rate_limits: Varies by plan; typically 1000 requests/minute per application; individual provider limits may also apply

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: Error responses include a type field and message, but underlying provider errors (e.g., Google quota exceeded) are sometimes surfaced without clear mapping to Nylas error codes, requiring agents to handle both Nylas and upstream error patterns.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: No idempotency key support on event creation or email send. Agents should check for existing events before creating to avoid duplicates on retry.
known_agent_gotchas: Grant IDs expire if the connected user revokes OAuth access — agents must handle 401 errors and prompt user re-auth
Provider-side rate limits (Google, Microsoft) can surface as Nylas errors — distinguish Nylas limits from upstream limits
Calendar event IDs are provider-specific and not portable — do not assume the same event ID works across providers
Availability queries can be slow (2-5s) when checking multiple calendars across providers with slow upstreams
Webhook delivery is not guaranteed and can lag — agents relying on real-time calendar changes should poll as a fallback

security_score_components.security_notes: OAuth grant model is secure; API keys should be kept server-side only. SOC2 and HIPAA certified. Data transits through Nylas infrastructure which is a consideration for sensitive email content.

score_rationale: Nylas is the best option when an agent must support multiple calendar/email providers through one API. The unified abstraction saves significant integration work. However, the multi-hop architecture (agent -> Nylas -> provider) adds latency and a dependency layer. Auth complexity is higher than single-provider APIs due to the OAuth grant flow per user. Rate limits are not transparently documented and upstream provider limits can surface unpredictably.
